notes_added: Treat registry products as replaceable delivery interfaces; bind release authority to a project-owned digest ledger and independently restorable bytes.
Measure sovereignty through successful restore from a separate failure domain, not through control of a registry process alone.
notes_challenged: Owning a self-hosted registry process alone proves artifact sovereignty.

available: primary technical sources for OCI distribution and registry operation
unavailable: reliably enumerable prior public-safe receipt inventory

agreements: Two copies count as independent only when they occupy separate failure domains.
Restore verification determines whether custody claims survive.
disagreements: Reader A initially treated an offline copy as the defining requirement; Reader B argued that a continuously replicated second site can satisfy the same control objective when failure independence is demonstrated.
omissions_detected: No current benchmark establishes artifact volume, publish rate, restore-time objective, or available upstream bandwidth.
No key-management artifact yet signs custody ledgers.
revisions_after_discussion: The design records failure-domain identity explicitly in the next schema revision rather than inferring independence from different paths.
The registry remains an OCI-compatible access plane while the custody ledger and verified bytes remain canonical.

implementation.capability: canonical artifact custody and restore authority

privacy.review: Passed automated field validation and manual exclusion review; contains no transcript excerpts, personal facts, locations, credentials, health information, animal information, or uniquely identifying fragments.

background.attention_bias: tracks commitments, handoffs, and whether recovery remains possible after authority disappears
background.counter_bias: does not equate long possession with verified integrity
background.experience: maintained custody chains through handoffs, damaged media, and incomplete manifests

observations: The request repeatedly converts provider independence into lifecycle ownership.
Rollback language carries stronger meaning when the bytes remain independently recoverable.
readers.thesis: The platform owns artifact history only when each release survives provider loss through a verified custody chain and an exercised restore path.
uncertainties: The available corpus does not expose every prior artifact-custody decision.

background.attention_bias: searches for shared failure domains, mutable names, and untested recovery assumptions
background.counter_bias: does not reject simple local storage when evidence shows bounded scale and independent backups
background.experience: tested bit rot, quorum loss, garbage-collection races, and misleading redundancy claims

observations: Content addressing supports portable identity across registry implementations.
Registry garbage collection can corrupt active uploads unless writes stop, so custody reachability and recovery verification gate deletion.
readers.thesis: An OCI registry provides useful interoperability but fails as sovereign authority unless digest-pinned ledger state and verified copies remain recoverable outside its tag database and operational process.
uncertainties: Production throughput and retention volume remain unmeasured.
